title: Coping with Peak Cypress and Willow Pollen Across Cities
summary: Spring pollen from cypress and willow trees causes allergies in Chinese cities, prompting both individual protective measures and urban solutions.

levels.title: Spring Pollen Causes Allergies
levels.content:
Spring has arrived in many Chinese cities. The weather is warm and trees are growing. But many people feel uncomfortable. They sneeze and their eyes feel itchy. Why? The answer is pollen.

Pollen comes from trees like cypress and willow. People cannot see it, but it is in the air. When people breathe it, they may get allergies. Young and middle-aged people often have this problem because they spend more time outside.

What can people do? They can wear masks and stay inside when pollen is high. They should also sleep well and eat healthy food. Cities are helping too. Beijing uses special spray to reduce pollen. Some cities are also planting different trees that make less pollen.

vocabulary.explanation: tiny powder from plants that can cause allergies
to suddenly push air out through your nose
having an uncomfortable feeling that makes you want to scratch

quiz.question: Why do many people feel uncomfortable in spring?
options: Because the weather is too cold
Because pollen in the air causes allergies
Because they eat unhealthy food

levels.title: Cities Battle Spring Pollen Allergies
levels.content:
As spring arrives in Chinese cities, a familiar problem returns: pollen allergies. While the warm weather and budding trees signal the season's arrival, many residents experience sneezing, itchy eyes, and nasal discomfort. The main culprits are cypress and willow trees, which release large amounts of pollen into the air.

According to health experts, pollen allergies are essentially an overreaction of the immune system to harmless substances. Young and middle-aged adults are particularly affected because their immune systems are more reactive and they spend more time outdoors. The symptoms can last for weeks during peak pollen season, typically from late March to early April.

Experts recommend several protective measures. People should avoid outdoor activities when pollen levels are high, wear masks, and use air purifiers indoors. Maintaining good sleep, a balanced diet, and regular exercise can also strengthen immunity and reduce allergy risks.

Cities are taking action too. Beijing has developed a pollen fixation agent that helps pollen settle faster, proving 17 to 20 times more effective than traditional water spraying. Additionally, urban planners are replacing high-pollen male cypress trees with female varieties that don't produce airborne pollen.25:T789,As spring unfolds across Chinese cities, the annual pollen season brings more than just blooming trees and warmer weather. For a significant portion of urban residents, it marks the onset of allergic symptomsâsneezing, itchy eyes, and nasal congestionâtriggered primarily by cypress and willow pollen. According to the China Meteorological Administration, cypress pollen has dominated since March, with willow pollen spreading across North China.

Unlike visible air pollution, pollen operates as an almost invisible seasonal health concern. Zhao Xiyao, a postgraduate student in Beijing, describes the experience: "In the morning and evening, it feels like there's a fine layer of dust in the air. As soon as I step outside, I start sneezing." Her coping strategyâtemporary relief through eye drops and nasal sprayâreflects a common "endure and wait" mentality among sufferers.

Medical experts explain that allergic reactions represent an excessive immune response to typically harmless substances. Young and middle-aged adults face heightened vulnerability due to more reactive immune systems and greater outdoor exposure. Luo Chaofan, a rehabilitation therapist, emphasizes that sustainable coping mechanismsâincluding adequate sleep, balanced nutrition, and regular exerciseâprove more effective than temporary fixes.

Meanwhile, urban governance is evolving to incorporate pollen management. Beijing has introduced a pollen fixation agent that accelerates particle settling with 17 to 20 times greater efficiency than conventional water spraying, requiring application only once daily or every two days. Long-term strategies include replacing high-allergen male cypress trees with female varieties that produce no airborne pollen, thereby maintaining aesthetic greenery while reducing public health risks. These initiatives signal a shift from individual adaptation toward systematic environmental intervention.8:["$","$L23",null,{"news":{"_id":"aa91cb6369c0f18c0000a08f14992ddf","title":"Coping with Peak Cypress and Willow Pollen Across Cities","titleid":"coping-with-peak-cypress-and-willow-pollen-across-cities","summary":"Spring pollen from cypress and willow trees causes allergies in Chinese cities, prompting both individual protective measures and urban solutions.","levels":[{"level":1,"title":"Spring Pollen Causes Allergies","content":"Spring has arrived in many Chinese cities. Young and middle-aged people often have this problem because they spend more time outside.\n\nWhat can people do? They can wear masks and stay inside when pollen is high. They should also sleep well and eat healthy food. Cities are helping too. Beijing uses special spray to reduce pollen. Some cities are also planting different trees that make less pollen.","vocabulary":[{"word":"pollen","explanation":"tiny powder from plants that can cause quiz.question: Why are young and middle-aged adults more affected by pollen allergies?
options: Because they have weaker immune systems than children
Because their immune systems are more reactive and they are outdoors more often
Because they don't use air purifiers at home

quiz.question: What long-term solution are cities implementing to reduce pollen?
options: Cutting down all cypress and willow trees
Replacing male cypress trees with female varieties that produce no airborne pollen
Spraying water on trees five times every day

levels.title: Urban Strategies Emerge to Combat Seasonal Pollen Allergies
levels.content:
As spring unfolds across Chinese cities, the annual pollen season brings more than just blooming trees and warmer weather. For a significant portion of urban residents, it marks the onset of allergic symptomsâsneezing, itchy eyes, and nasal congestionâtriggered primarily by cypress and willow pollen. According to the China Meteorological Administration, cypress pollen has dominated since March, with willow pollen spreading across North China.

Unlike visible air pollution, pollen operates as an almost invisible seasonal health concern. Zhao Xiyao, a postgraduate student in Beijing, describes the experience: "In the morning and evening, it feels like there's a fine layer of dust in the air. As soon as I step outside, I start sneezing." Her coping strategyâtemporary relief through eye drops and nasal sprayâreflects a common "endure and wait" mentality among sufferers.

Medical experts explain that allergic reactions represent an excessive immune response to typically harmless substances. Young and middle-aged adults face heightened vulnerability due to more reactive immune systems and greater outdoor exposure. Luo Chaofan, a rehabilitation therapist, emphasizes that sustainable coping mechanismsâincluding adequate sleep, balanced nutrition, and regular exerciseâprove more effective than temporary fixes.

Meanwhile, urban governance is evolving to incorporate pollen management. Beijing has introduced a pollen fixation agent that accelerates particle settling with 17 to 20 times greater efficiency than conventional water spraying, requiring application only once daily or every two days. Long-term strategies include replacing high-allergen male cypress trees with female varieties that produce no airborne pollen, thereby maintaining aesthetic greenery while reducing public health risks. quiz.question: What distinguishes pollen from industrial pollution according to the article?
options: Pollen is almost invisible yet accumulates as a seasonal health concern
Pollen only affects people with chronic conditions
Pollen can be completely eliminated through water spraying

quiz.question: Why does Luo Chaofan recommend sustainable lifestyle habits over temporary fixes?
options: Because pollen allergies are recurrent and seasonal, lasting weeks or longer
Because eye drops and nasal sprays are too expensive for most people
Because outdoor activities should be completely avoided during spring

quiz.question: What advantage does the pollen fixation agent offer compared to traditional methods?
options: It completely eliminates all pollen from the environment permanently
It is 17 to 20 times more efficient and requires less frequent application
It changes male cypress trees into female trees naturally

title: Natural Doesn't Always Mean Better: Understanding the Appeal to Nature
summary: The naturalistic fallacy misleads us into believing natural products are always superior to synthetic ones.

levels.title: Natural Is Not Always Better
levels.content:
Many people think natural things are always good. But this is not true.

Some natural things can hurt us. Arsenic is natural, but it can kill people. Cyanide is also natural. It comes from some plants like almonds and peaches. These things are dangerous.

Some man-made things help us. Medicines save lives. Glasses help us see better. Refrigerators keep our food fresh. These things make our lives better.

When someone says a product is "natural," think carefully. Ask why it is better. Natural does not always mean safe or good.

vocabulary.explanation: coming from nature, not made by people
able to cause harm or injury
machines that keep food cold and fresh

quiz.question: According to the article, why should we think carefully when someone says a product is natural?
options: Because natural products are always expensive
Because natural does not always mean safe or good
Because natural things come from plants only

levels.title: The Problem With Natural Products
levels.content:
Companies often advertise their products as "natural." They want us to believe natural is always better. But this idea has a problem.

Many natural things are harmful. Arsenic and asbestos are natural, but they cause serious health problems. Some plants produce cyanide, a deadly poison. Even "natural" baby products have caused seizures because they contained belladonna, a poisonous plant.

Meanwhile, synthetic products have improved our lives greatly. Modern medicine has made childbirth much safer. Vaccinations have saved millions of lives by preventing diseases like smallpox and whooping cough. We process and cook food to make it safer and more nutritious.

The word "natural" is poorly defined. It doesn't guarantee that a product is better or safer. Next time you see this claim, ask for real evidence. Don't just accept that something is good because it's called natural.

vocabulary.explanation: to tell people about a product to make them buy it
made by people, not found in nature
sudden uncontrolled movements of the body
to promise that something is certain or true

quiz.question: What does the article say about the word "natural" in product marketing?
options: It is clearly defined and always means the product is organic
It is poorly defined and doesn't guarantee safety or quality
It can only be used for food products

quiz.question: Which example does the article use to show that synthetic products can be beneficial?
options: Vaccinations have saved millions of lives from diseases
Natural remedies are more effective than modern medicine
Synthetic materials are always cheaper than natural ones

levels.title: Understanding the Naturalistic Fallacy in Modern Marketing
levels.content:
The "appeal to nature," or naturalistic fallacy, is a pervasive logical flaw used by influencers, brands, and politicians. It suggests that natural products are inherently superior to synthetic alternatives, but this reasoning is fundamentally flawed.

Philosopher John Stuart Mill highlighted this problem in 1874. If we accept that everything natural is good, we must embrace all of nature's creationsâincluding arsenic, asbestos, and deadly diseases like smallpox, which killed 300-500 million people in the 20th Century alone. Conversely, many synthetic innovations have dramatically improved human welfare. Modern medicine reduced maternal mortality from one in 100 to one in 10,000 in industrialized nations.

The term "natural" itself is poorly defined and context-dependent. Fluoride, often perceived as synthetic, is actually a naturally occurring mineral, while nano-hydroxyapatite, marketed as a natural alternative in toothpaste, is laboratory-made. This ambiguity makes "natural" an effective marketing tool precisely because it exploits our cognitive biases.

When evaluating products or arguments, we should demand evidence beyond mere appeals to nature. The question isn't whether something is natural, but whether it's demonstrably effective and safe.8:["$","$L23",null,{"news":{"_id":"aa91cb6369b5f2fe000013645bcd57bb","title":"Natural Doesn't Always Mean Better: Understanding the Appeal to Nature","titleid":"natural-doesnt-always-mean-better-understanding-the-appeal-to-nature","summary":"The naturalistic fallacy misleads us into believing natural products are always superior to synthetic ones.","levels":[{"level":1,"title":"Natural Is Not Always Better","content":"Many people think natural things are always good. Some man-made things help us. Medicines save lives. Glasses help us see better. Refrigerators keep our food fresh. These things make our lives better.

When someone says a product is "natural," think carefully. Ask why it is better. quiz.question: What does the fluoride and nano-hydroxyapatite example illustrate?
options: People often misidentify which substances are natural versus synthetic
Fluoride is more dangerous than its alternatives
